sentences.text: Correct.
Not really.
We had no-- we kept that separated, and I have no knowledge of particulars of what was going on there.
No.
I felt-- first of all, I felt awful, specifically when it was a Jewish person, which was most of the time-- that one brother can do this to another brother.
And as far as for practical purposes, all I was doing was trying to protect my body that the hurt should be the least-- I mean, with my hands, covering my face and so forth.
Of course, most of the time when you were beaten, in many, many of these cases, they would make you bend down and take 25 lashes.
So it wasn't so simple to protect yourself from that.
Yes.
As a matter of fact-- I'm coming back now to the-- to the discussion of Lublin Majdanek.
And I mentioned that before, to you, that our whole-- doing was going around faith, praying, whenever you had the chance.
Within a week that I was there-- and I constantly had my prayer book in the hand.
Whenever I could, I was saying the Psalms at the-- at the place, and I was caught by one of the SS guards.
And then he took me in front of all the guys there, and he made me bend down.
And he says he's going to give me 25 lashes, and I have to count.
And trying to maintain a little kind of a humor in the misery, in the middle of the counting, as much as it was hurting, I uttered some Jewish words, which was a cuss word towards him.
And all the guys who were assembled to watch there, broke out into laughter.
And he wanted to know what happened.
Of course, nobody said what happened.
But most of the time they loved-- they enjoyed made us count the lashes.
Of course, in the middle of the count, you also had the yelling, oh, it hurts, and so forth.
So bestial, as they were, they enjoyed that.
No.
I recall in Birkenau.
I remember the times when two particular guys ran away.
I forgot their names.
They wrote a book about it.
It was the only, I think-- at least what I think-- the only two guys who ran off and made it, and really notified the world on what's going on.
At that time, of course, that was for them, devastating to the Germans.

So we were staying in appell-- which is the line-up-- for the whole afternoon, the whole evening through the middle of the next day, while they were going around with the dogs to try to find them, until they called it off.
Because to us, it meant extreme punishments.
If they can't find them, you know, they gave us the portion, to teach us a lesson what happens to you the next time if somebody runs away.
During the time when you were in Kanada, did you ever-- as far as the transports that would come in and stuff, do you ever remember seeing or hearing of a transport from your hometown?
No.
There was no such a thing anymore, because my-- my hometown was emptied out in 1942 and in 1943.
So all the transports that came in right now, they came in from-- from Hungary.
And we, in Kanada, itself, had no contact whatsoever with people.
Every once in a while, maybe, somebody came up with a suitcase, with a name that somebody may-- might have known, but it was very rare.
But they had some-- they had some people-- I recall now, they had a woman in the Kanada who-- our contact with women there was-- was very liberal.
In other words, there was no prohibition to talk to a girl, because in the course of our work, we-- we met the women.
You know, they were usually sorting stuff.
We brought it to them and so forth.
So contact with a woman was not prohibited as such.
Fraternizing was, but-- and this woman-- once she got to know me-- once told me that she wants-- she wants to divulge something to me, but she is very much scared.
I was kind of surprised.
I said, why are you scared?
What do you have to tell me?
What is so secretive?
She said what she has to tell me can cost her her life.
I said, why would you then tell me that?
Don't forget, I'm-- I'm only-- I didn't even turn 20 at that time.
And she was a little bit older.
I don't remember how much older, but maybe she was 25, 26.
She says, I can't bear it anymore in my chest.
It's a secret that can cost me my life if they find out.
But I have to share it with somebody.
OK, I said.
What's the secret?
She originally comes from Humenne, which is a neighboring town from Michalovce.
And she was taken away at the same time my sister was taken away, when the first girls went away and was taken to Auschwitz.
And she tells me that in 1942, in the beginning of 1942, within two months that she came to Auschwitz, she ran away.
She escaped, and she made it back.
She came back to the city that they had taken her from, and she begins going around telling people of the mass murder that's going on in Birkenau and Auschwitz.
And everybody looks at her, that she has lost her mind.
Nobody would believe.
It came to such a point that the people were telling her-- she used to be-- not like a maid, but like a helping hand to somebody's house.
And she was trying to put the whole effort to explain to these people who knew her about what's going on.
They thought that-- she, or their family, of course, would believe her.
And they wouldn't believe her.
And it came to a point where they told her that if she doesn't stop spreading around such false rumors, that they will take her and lock her up, and put her in a psychiatric hospital.
It's unbelievable.
When she talks to me, I look in bewilderment.
What are you talking about?
This is true?
But you can see from this, on the inhumanity that they were subjecting people to, that a normal thinking human being would never believe that this can happen-- with mass graves, mass gassing.
So she says, she escaped to Hungary, because she was afraid that her fellow Jews would commit her to a sanitarium-- to an asylum.
She escaped to Hungary, and there she lived in safety until 1944.
And now she came back with the Hungarian transport for the second time.
She says that if the Nazis find that out, she'd be put to death in a second, because there is no bigger sin by the Nazis to escape a concentration camp.
So she says, I cannot live with the thought by myself.
I have to share it with somebody.
So she entrusted this to me.
And it was-- you know, I felt good about it.
And she felt like a relief.
I don't carry that burden now by myself.
There is somebody else that shares it with me.
It gives you--it gives you a little picture, even to me today, how much belief does a person have?
And I speak to them on the street, or in the train, or in a plane-- and I tell them, look, I was in Auschwitz, and this and this happened.
He probably looks at me bewildered.
Come on, don't-- I mean, the world has read now, and saw in pictures enough to know better.
But it's-- it's not plausible.
It's not plausible to believe that something like this can happen to you.
To me, it was-- it was even bigger news, was I never knew of the fact-- because we-- I was in the men's camp, not in the women's camp.
Because we had to stay around for appell if a man disappeared.
And the women had to stand around if the women-- but I never-- to me, the case wasn't known that a woman escaped Auschwitz, and she arrived.
So you have to realize, also, that-- that we were subjected to all the punishments because somebody ran away.
We endured the punishment with happiness-- we were-- we were happy that somebody escapes.
And there was a chance in a million that the world outside will find out, and-- and maybe something will be done about it.
You know, I recall now that sometime in 1943, in Birkenau-- I don't remember what period of time that was-- I received a package from the Swedish Red Cross-- two cans of sardines.
The sardines meant the world to me in 1943.
Meant the world to me.
But the fact that I saw my name in print, that somebody in the world knows that Manny Mittelman is locked up in Auschwitz, meant to me much more than the two cans of the sardines, of course.
So in other words, it gives you a ray of hope.
Wait a minute-- somebody may be working in your behalf.
The world knows.
You have a chance, maybe, maybe.
Very little.
We knew from seeping in-- as I mentioned to you before.
There were always some civilians coming into the camp.
And we knew some kinds of-- let's see, it was the end of 1944 in Birkenau.
We could hear, already, shooting.
We didn't know what it was until, through rumors, we find out the Russians are coming, and they're so close already.
We knew that Germany's on-- on the verge of-- not complete collapse, but we knew that they were being surrounded, from the little rumors that came in through the civilians.
You know-- you see this through a sieve.

We were, of course, looking forward to a liberation, but we were also more scared that there would never be a liberation because nobody, in our minds, nobody ever believed they would let us come out and be live witnesses to tell the world.
And when we get into the next segment about leaving Birkenau, you'll find out the reason why we acted the way we did act.
Because every person judges the people who are trying to torture you by their previous experiences.
And by the previous experiences, you knew that a German does not want a Jew to come out in the world alive to be able to tell this story.
That was their main purpose.
For some reason, they were afraid.
They were afraid of pressure, of world pressure, or whatever it was.
OK, so we're now in the end of 1944, and we're beginning-- we already hear the-- like I said, the artillery from the Russians.
That might have been yet a few days away.
And there is an announcement coming into the camps that they're going to evacuate Birkenau, Auschwitz.
So they come out, and they give us certain kind of a rules or regulations about when it's going to be, and how it's going to be, and so forth.

So we had a council of-- if I may call it, the council of sages, of old timers, of the guys who were already in Auschwitz three years, who know it all, who are-- who know the psychology of the Germans, how they would act, react to any kind of an incident.
And we have a meeting one day.
You know, it didn't have to be secret, because once you were in the camp, the Germans were outside.
So we weren't worried about this meeting.
But there were chosen, maybe 20, 25 guys, all of them who were already longer, or about two and a half, three years in Auschwitz.
None of them who were in this council-- if I call it that for a purpose-- were any newcomer Hungarians, because we thought, what do they know about the Germans?
We know better.
So we have this meeting, we decide, because we already knew that the Germans are going to give us bread.
They're going to give us drinks, and they're going to give us salami.
And they're going to tell us, you'll get food galore.
You're going to go on this big march.
Because they had no vehicles, they were in such trouble to transport us.
So they wanted to bring us to a city that is called Gleiwitz, which is not too far away.
I think it's about 25 miles from Auschwitz.
But they had to evacuate the whole camp.
So we discussed, now, with these guys, what do we do?
We can find a hiding place.
We can dig some graves and hide in the graves and prepare food and prepare-- we have plenty of clothing in here.
So we were trying to evaluate the situation and trying to find somebody to come up with a good idea.
This is a chance of our life.
We knew that in a very short time the Russians will be here.
And maybe we have a chance to be liberated.
So everybody voiced his opinion, but the majority all came up with the same verdict.
They says, by the time you evacuate the camp, the Germans will come in with machine guns.
They'll kill every sick person.
All we have to do, let's say, is go to the infirmary and say we're sick.
And they would have taken us.
All you had to do is report there.
And in our quote, unquote, 'wisdom,' and past experiences, we all decided that the silliest thing would be to try to bury yourself, hide yourself, go to a hospital.
Because nobody alive will come out of this camp.
They'll go through there, and they'll come through it, and they'll raze it, and make like a field, you know, that has grown for years nothing but-- but kind of produce or whatever.
There won't be a sign that there was Auschwitz.
We were all almost unanimous of that.
Whoever wasn't, took a different approach.
There were guys-- I remember I had a friend who came from my hometown.
He's still alive.
He lives here in town.
He was an American citizen, and wasn't taken to Auschwitz until 1944.
And he came in around-- I don't know, maybe in the midst of '44 to Auschwitz, to Birkenau.
When he found out I'm there, he sent a message for me, because he wanted to get some benefit of an old timer.
And I met him.
And one day, he notifies me that they're taking him away for a transport to a different camp.
Could I help him stay there?
He wants to be close to me.
So I went to the supervisor there, and said, I was an old timer-- we usually had a little pull there.
I says, listen, maybe you can take this kid out, and substitute somebody else for him.
I know him from home, and so forth.
He says, I don't understand Jews.
You're talking so foolish.
You know none of us will come out alive here.
Maybe the place where he's going he has a chance in a million to stay alive.
Why would you deny a person a chance like this?
So it dawned on me, maybe the guy is right.
So I went back to the fellow, and I told him, look, he promised me that he will take you out.
That's all I can tell you.
But he didn't-- of course, he didn't.
He left.
He was liberated in January, a month later.
And we met after the war, in Bratislava, and he hugged me on the street and kept on yelling, you saved my life by not having him taken out of there.
And-- and I went through hell from now on.
OK, let's get back to the subject matter.
We have this meeting, and we decide, no, we're all going.
So what we did decide though, that every one of us will have under our concentration camp uniform, civilian clothes, and be ready on the road to escape whenever the chance is given.
You never know what's happening.
So they gave us-- the Germans gave us food galore, whatever you wanted.
I mean, from whatever they had, whatever you wanted you could take.
They had salami.
It was horse salami-- OK, it doesn't make a difference.
At that time, it was delicious.
Bread, as much as you want.
Canteens, even we had, you know, to take a long water.
And in January 1945, I don't remember exactly the day it was.
I think 17, 18, 19, maybe about-- we started the death march.
And I don't have to tell you the reason why they call it the death march-- is because most of the people didn't make it to get there.
Don't forget, we come from Kanada.
So we were already well fed for a little while.
And we have, again, all the strength gathered back together, so we had a little more power than the rest of the guys who were there.
So arriving to Gleiwitz, on the road, people were shot and killed one after another, whoever couldn't make it, you know.
The guy just pulled his rifle off.
It sounds maybe so cold of me, the way I say it, but it was nothing to them.
You fell down.
Down and out, that's it.
At that time, of course, they didn't make us take the dead ones along, because at that time, they didn't care anymore who it was.
So, by the time we arrived to Gleiwitz, which was three days-- we walked three days and three nights-- the Russians were in Auschwitz.
And everybody who stayed there was liberated in Birkenau.
Not one person died.
Not the infirmary, not the ones who were hiding.
It was-- I mean, after the war, it was-- we felt like fools, you know, that like we had freedom in our hands.
The Russians liberated these guys.
Anyway, we were in Gleiwitz.
And in Gleiwitz they let us sleep over.
I remember we slept-- I don't remember.
I think 36 hours or something.
I remember everybody was beat, I mean.
After that, they gave us again-- and they resumed giving us all kinds of foods.
And they loaded us in trains, cattle trains.
And we began a new epoch, so to say, a new way of life, all different from what we were used to in the last few months.
And we're heading now-- running away from the Russians, we're heading into deep Germany with the trains.
Now, again, you're piled up there.
I don't recall now, but I remember I took from Auschwitz a pair of shoes that were real high shoes.
You know, they lace up almost to the middle of your foot.
And I had good socks and everything.
The only mistake I made, that in the night I did not take the shoes off, so my feet froze.
Because you're talking about January in Gleiwitz.
That is January, February in Detroit with 10 below.
That's the kind of temperature you're talking about-- unheated cattle train.
I mean, the only pleasure we had is hugging, maybe, each other to get a little warmth.
But it was awful.
It was awful.
They dragged us, I don't recall exactly, but two or three days they dragged us into deep Germany.
And we came to a city that is called Zolstadt.
Zolstadt is a little landmark on the map.
I remember when I was in Yad Vashem, I couldn't find it.
They have a map of all concentration camps.
I was very upset-- I couldn't find the camp Zolstadt.

The guy later explained to me that it was a real small camp, where they were making-- the Germans were producing the V2 and the V3s-- if you remember the rockets, against England that they were shooting out?
And this, they had it all on the ground of that camp.
So they brought us to Zolstadt.
I stayed there three days.
And the whole Birkenau 1942 started repeating itself-- the beatings.
I mean, the dogs-- it-- it was almost like new to us.
All of a sudden, we're being mistreated again after-- after the little relief we had in the Kanada.

And of course, we started crying, literally, all of our guys that we made such a stupid-- we would have been better to die in Auschwitz-- we didn't know that they were liberated-- than to go through all this hell again.
What for?
Then they took us from there.
I stayed there are only three days.
It was terrible-- beatings, I mean, day and night, day and night.
They took us from there to a second camp which was called Osterode.
Funny German words-- it was deep in Germany.
Again, underground V2 stuff, and they were strict.
Like I said, it brought back all the picture, that the-- and all this sub-human treatment that we used to get in the 1942 when we first arrived to Birkenau.
For some reason-- I don't know what happened-- that for some reason we couldn't stay in this camp for too long of a period of time.
And it didn't happen to all of us.

They eventually detailed us on a train, again, on a cattle train, with a commandant who was anti-Semitic, where every second he had a chance, he said, I will kill all the Jews on this train before the end comes.
We have to realize, now we're talking about, already, the American army encircling on one side, the English army the other side.
Germany is growing smaller and smaller.
And we are detailed on this train, with a commandant.
And he takes his private two horses around on this train.
And we are subject now to live out of air, because it's not-- there is no place where you can draw food or drinks.
So when we used to come from station to station-- water we used to get from the station, itself.
You know, the station had water to fill up the locomotives at that time.
And I'm talking about an era, you know, where the locomotives were fed with coal and water.
And they were going by steam, and so water we had, when we came to the station.
That's the only time.
But food was scarce.
I mean, you were eating the nails from your body.
It was-- actually, there was none where they could get to it, either.
And we were hopping around from city to city.
And the misery got bigger and bigger and bigger.
So all the-- the weight that I gained in that time in the Kanada was, in no time, lost.
And you were down to decimated-- I mean, what are called in Germany, a musselman.
Nothing.
It was all gone.
And one day we arrived-- we arrived in a-- we went through Ceske Katowice.
I mean, unbelievable as-- as it was, I come to a city-- I only knew about Katowice in Poland.
But there is a Czech Katowice.
And we were on the train where the main station was, and all of a sudden American fliers are arriving, thinking in their mind that this is an army train, and start diving at us and shooting.
So all hell broke loose.
Everybody was-- jumped out.
They opened up the-- the-- you know, the wagons there, let us jump out, to take some protection there.
So there were-- on one side was all fields, and one side was all houses.
So both the SS, the supervising thing, and everybody else, ran out.
It was a free for all like, because they were shooting one after another.
And I jumped out of the train, and I hid under a bridge upon which that train just passed, which usually has these big planks for carrying the load of the train, itself.
You know the railroad-- what do you call those railroad planks that have really about six by six, eight by eight?
Real heavy stuff.
And on top of this, heavy wood, because it has to stand the load of the train.
And myself and a friend of mine who had the number-- I still remember his name-- Ini Popel.
And he had-- I had 04-37-014.
He had 37-016.
Both of us are hiding under that bridge, and trying to protect ourselves from the bullets.
So in the meanwhile, some of us were daring and taking jackets off from the concentration camp-- striped jackets-- and throwing it out on the top of the train.
So we thought that the-- the Americans will see that this is-- these are guys who are locked up, so they'll stop shooting.
Of course, it didn't help any.
I mean, what can you see from a plane?
That this is a uniform from a guy who's-- who's in Auschwitz?
It didn't make any-- I don't know what they thought about when they saw these jackets flying.
And I was lying underneath there, and the bullets were flying around me-- I mean, by the tenth.
And I said to my friend, Ini, I don't think this is a safe place.
Look at these bullets-- they're piercing the wood.
They're flying down upon our head-- and look at how many we have around us.
I says, this is not a safe place to stay.
I said, let's run away.
We're going to go away to some other place.
And he said to me, no, I'm staying here.
They can never penetrate wood like this.
But you see, you see the bullets flying!
I said, you can stay.
I'm leaving.
And I picked myself up, and I started running through the field.
And I remember it as vividly as today, because I repeated to my daughter several times, if there was ever a guardian angel in my life, he was guarding me from these bullets.
There was a plane following me and shooting at me.
And I was using the theory that if you lie down on the ground, there is much more substance of you that he can catch.
There is more of you.
You're spread about the floor.
I mean, it's a false theory.
But if I'm standing, they only have the width of my body to shoot me.
I kept on running.
And the bullets were flying around me like somebody pours rice on a bride at a wedding.
I swear to you that I could probably technically catch them in my hands.
In an amazement of thinking all this time, how come I'm still--I'm still running?
I felt-- I felt them on my body, actually.
And nothing happened to me.
And eventually I ended up in a chicken coop by a-- by a peasant there someplace, you know, on a farm.
And I was completely out of breath.
And the chickens were all there, and I was hungry like a horse-- of course, from no food.
And there are a bunch of eggs that the chicken laid.
And I started opening these eggs, one after another, raw, and-- and drank them.
In-- for about an hour or so, I sit there, and waiting what's going to happen.
And everything quieted down.
